rationale: The citing paper references Srivastava and Chakravarti (2009) for a specific empirical claim, not just as a background list item: communication about intentions helps only when the discloser has a high trustworthiness reputation. That finding is used in the theory section to motivate the paper's core argument about why disclosure often fails under asymmetry unless credibility is established. The authors then develop and test a related but new condition, arguing that verifiability of disclosed cost in information-symmetric front-end settings can generate trust and improve downstream outcomes.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Srivastava and Chakravarti's contingency view of communication and trust in negotiation. It extends that line by proposing verifiability-driven early cost disclosure as an alternative trust-building mechanism and testing its effects on front-end and back-end profits.
